speaker: interviewer
text: OK. October 19, 1995. Continuing with Marcel Baum. Interviewed by [? Ita ?] [? Gordon. ?] We're in Westminster, California, USA. Speaking English. Take number two. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that to you left before Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The was in June of 1939--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and I. Just my sister and I went to France to our grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And our parents had to stay in-- in Germany because their paper-- their paperwork was not done in France to-- to immigrate into France. But we were children so my grandparent covered-- covered that up. So in 19-- in November of 1938, finally our parents arrived to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Kristallnacht. After the Kristallnacht, yes. They didn't have anything. And everything was broken. And, you know, there was not much left. So they came to France. And in the meantime, at the age of 14, I started to get a job, already. In-- in Europe, you know, you-- you can become an apprentice as soon you finish school. So I became a baker, an apprentice baker. And I worked in a bakery shop in the same town where my grandparents lived. And started working at the age of 14. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my parents came in November of '38. And my grandparents got them an apartment. And my father would work for my grandparents, do some kind of chores, as-- as much as he could, you know. And-- in 19-- then in 1939, when the war broke out with Poland, my parents already got the notice because they were German, that they should not stay in [INAUDIBLE]. They should flee, go someplace in hiding, someplace not really hiding but not stay so close to the borderline. So my parents moved to Limoges.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Limoges is in the Haute-Vienne. That's the southern part of France. And in 1939, when the war broke out, my-- my boss was called to the army. I had to be a boss already at the age of 15, to bake all the bread, four to five oven of bread daily, and help in the afternoon to distribute the bread to farmers. In-- in Europe, the farmers would not pay for the bread. They would give them grain, you know, their own grain. And then we would bake the bread and distribute to them. They would pay-- instead of money, they would pay with grains. So we would distribute around the farm-- around the towns, in-- the bread and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city was that what, what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was still in Vic-sur-Seille.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Vic-sur-Seille.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my-- when my parents had left to Limoges in 1940, when the German started advancing to Holland and [INAUDIBLE] and coming to Paris, it became dangerous for me to live in Vic, so I was going to join my parents in Limoges for being more safe. But unfortunately, I left Vic-sur-Seille on July the 11th to go to Limoges.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940. And I came to-- to Paris and certainly no train were going out of Paris to Limoges because they were-- Germans were advancing from-- from Belgium, and into-- going into France, because the Germans wanted to be in France on the 14th of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? The Germans loved Paris. Paris was an open city. They-- they wouldn't bomb Paris because of all the beauty, you know, everything. That's one thing. But Hitler always wanted to see Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I was in Paris on-- on July the 12th. Stayed overnight in a hotel. I always had one uncle who was very generous. He always gave me money. When I left, he gave me 500 franc. And that was a lot of money in these days in 1940, 500 franc. So he said, in case of an emergency, you should have money. So he gave me that money. So I stayed overnight in a hotel. I had two big suitcases with clothes for my parents, actually, and other things and my clothes. When I heard the-- that the German were coming into Paris, I left the two suitcases. I said, I don't need this. And I-- I jumped on a train. It was not a regular train. It was a military and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] baggage train. You know? Baggage train, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took off. It-- on the train, it was-- said 'Limoges.' But I did not end up in Limoges. I ended up in Tours-- Tours, France. And then we-- we had to abandon the train because they couldn't go any further. There was bombing and things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So suddenly I went to Tours and stayed in Tours. Looked around in Tours. Found a bicycle shop and purchased myself a bicycle to go to Limoges. And that's about 200, I would say about 200 miles. I would like to backtrack a little bit. You mentioned that in Paris, you heard that the Germans were coming in. Who told you? How did you hear? How did you hear about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the street, the newspapers, and the radio. You know, you-- you hear through-- through people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you observe? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was fighting. And everybody wanted to get out of Paris, not only me. Even the French people wanted to get out of Paris. So we-- we did the best we can to leave Paris. So that's why I-- I-- O even was on the wrong-- Paris has four train stations, one was east, west, south. So I had to even find out which station to go to to go down to Limoges. So I took the subway and-- and went to the-- to the right station.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when you got on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, and that's when I got on the train. And we-- I-- we ended up, we went to Orleans and down to Tours. And suddenly the train stopped, and we couldn't go any place. Then we joined-- I-- I bought a bicycle and went on to bicycle. And suddenly after three days, I came to Limoges. And when I came to Limoges, not only was I surprised to find my parents, my grandparents had also left Vic-sur-Seille because the Germans were coming in. And my sister was with them, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the grandparents, and two uncles. And my mother and dad lived in one apartment. A kitchen, a living room, and a bedroom. So suddenly we were 10, 12 people there. And we all had to stay together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get to Limoges? Did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- my-- my-- my-- my grandparents had a-- a-- a truck, a small truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so they came with a truck. They came prepared, you see. They had clothes and things like this. And my-- and my youngest uncle was just released from the German prison, so he drove the truck down to Limoges.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like in Limoges and France, other than the tight living quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Limoges became a town where a lot of Jewish people moved to. I don't know why. I can't-- I couldn't tell you why. But you found a lot of Jews from Germany, from Luxembourg, from-- from Belgium, from all over, made a point to go to Limoges. They figure Limoges is in the center, so the Germans wouldn't get there. By there were a lot of European people in-- living in Limoges. So Limoges was not an easy life because, you know, when you don't have an income, and-- and my parents didn't have any more income. They had to depend on their-- on their parents and uncles. They had to live from them. We lost everything in Germany. So it was very, very difficult. But I find myself, again, a job in a-- in a bakery. Wasn't making much money. But a little money I made, at least, could help my parents out. We lived in Limoges for about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six months, close to a year. And it became we needed more space. You know, we couldn't find an apartment. Limoges was congested. So we moved house out north of Limoges to-- to Bonnac-la-Cote. And there my uncle and grandparents and my mother started a small business, like a-- a sandwich shop where we made lunches and breakfast just to support ourselves. And I would, again, find myself a job as a baker. I would work nights. And in France, you had to work from 2:00, 3 o'clock in the morning because they had to have nice fresh bread in the morning. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would find myself a job, again, and worked in the bakery. In the meantime, also, I would chase the girls a little bit. [LAUGHS] 18, 19-- 17, 18, 19 years old, I would go and buy the milk. You know, n France, you go every day with your little bucket and buy the milk. And so I got acquainted with two of the farmer's girls. And actually I liked one girl better than the other one. And I would go and visit there. I would go early for them, get the milk. The milk wasn't even ready for me, but I would go there early on. When I had nothing to do, I would go with her and sit and watch the cows and the animals they have to-- the-- when they ate the grass and stuff like this. And we got very well-acquainted. And very-- and very nice people. And they had a cousin who was a-- was in the Maquis. He was the head of the underground in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Maquis was called the underground. He was the head. So [? naturally. ?] And first when I met these people, and I told them I was Jewish, they did not know what a Jew was in-- in certain parts of France. They-- they figured the Jew was a different person. They imagined something altogether different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you treated differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I have to say I was treated very, very nicely. After they got to know me and how nice we were and how gentle we were, that we were people like they were, they are. Or that we were like they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't experience any anti-semitism when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Limoges, in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not even in Limoges in general, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to-- when you mentioned your grandparents were with you, were your father's, your second father's parents also with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My second father's parents, they died early.

speaker: interviewer
text: They died. What were their names? August?

speaker: interviewee
text: August Joseph--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mollie.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Mollie Joseph. Yeah, uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were-- they had--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had passed on before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you were with the-- the pretty girls. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so I made good friends. And we would communicate and go out and talk. You know, there wasn't much in France. Here and there, we would go to a little dance or something like this. And then in September of 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was it '40-- yeah, September of '42, we lived out there. My uncle, I had my uncle Fernand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the one--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the second-- the-- the second-- no, the third son of my grandparents. But he had a lot of asthma problem. So he was always sick, one way or another, always sick with asthma. But he was a self-educated man. Because of being so sick all the time, he had read and educated himself. So he was a good friends with the-- what do you call it here? Like a deputy, deputy.

speaker: interviewer
text: A congressman, a representative, representative?

speaker: interviewee
text: Representative. You would say that in French, a deputy, like a congressman, yes. He was a good friend with a congressman in Limoges. And this fellow heard that the German-- that the Germans were looking-- going to look for all the Jews in France, for all the Jews in the non-occupied France, because France was split. There was the occupied France and the non-occupied France. So we lived in the non-occupied France.

speaker: interviewer
text: So France was-- so he heard about that the Germans were going to look for all the German Jews in the non-occupied-- Territory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Territory, in Limoges, in [INAUDIBLE]. The Jews from France didn't have to worry about the German Jews. So he called him in the afternoon and told my uncle, make sure your sister and husband and the children get out of your house and see what you can do and hide them, if you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that afternoon, I went over to-- to those nice farmers and explained to them my situation, what's going to happen. That we had just a phone call from a friend of ours in Limoges that they were going to look for all the Jewish children and some Jewish families, more than that. So if they could take me for awhile over to stay with them. Naturally, you know, it's-- it's difficult, but they agreed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went to their cousin who-- and-- and explained it to their cousin, [? Jean. ?] He was in the Maquis. He said, you help these people as much you can. So we went-- I went to the farmer's house. And my parents had to walk about three kilometers to go to another home where [? Jean's ?] parents lived, the fellow from the Maquis, where his parents lived. And they were-- they hid my parents there. So-- so we were separated already. In the meantime, my sister was in Limoges with another family, hidden by another family. My brother was in the same house, hidden from the people who he was working for as a butcher. So we were all separated already and pretty well hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other two families where you stayed and your parents stayed, were they related? Did they know one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, they were cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were cousins.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's why it was so easy. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names, the family names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Senamaud. So Jean Senamaud was the cousin's name. And the other family was Senamaud, too. So-- [LAUGHS] so naturally, so I could-- so I stayed with these people for about one week because in Europe, they didn't have ceilings like here. They just had six by four and then your floor. And that's it. And that was all they have.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you walked, you could hear everything. So being in a farm where they sold milk to other people and things like this, the people were-- were getting very concerned that somebody maybe would hear me or-- or would hear what's going on in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, it was the harvest season. And when it's the harvest season, it's these farmers, they buy one machine. And they then do the harvesting from one farm to the other farm. And they were all like a collective. You know, they don't each one has their own big harvesting machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were afraid somebody would hear me in the-- in the house. So I then joined my parents by the other Senamaud family. And they-- that was over-- over a barn. But we had to stay three people in one room. In Europe, you did not have toilet facility. All you had is a little [LAUGHS] pot in the middle of the-- of the room. And you had to do your business.

speaker: interviewee
text: And naturally, my mother is a woman, and she-- you know, she put a curtain around her. And I was laying here in bed. And she was-- they were laying their in bed. And it became very difficult. I mean, we didn't suffer. We got three nice meals a day. The people were just wonderful to us. But then in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] April-- no, in November of '42, we were supposed to go to Switzerland, my brother and I. Some relative of ours made connection to go to Switzerland. And the day we were supposed to leave, that was cancelled. So I had to go back to my parents, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in-- were you able to talk to your brother during this time while you were in hiding? Was there any-- how did you communicate, through whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, through-- through our uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through your uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through our uncle and grandparents. They-- they-- you know, they-- then-- then finally a friend of ours, who-- who was actually my grandma's maid, they lived in southern part of France near Lourdes. You've heard about the city of Lourdes, where they heal all the people and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near Spain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Spain, yeah. Mhm. She came up to Limoges and told us that quite a few people now were crossing the border from France to Spain, and then go to the French consul. And then they can join the French Army or whatever, the-- the underground, again, in Morocco. So naturally, we-- we were getting restless, my brother and I. You know, can't be cooped up at 12, 14-- with 16, 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mr. Senamaud made us false identification. We got pictures made. We got-- he-- we-- he came and made up a-- made a picture of me, and my-- my brother had pictures made from somebody else. And we got false identification made. So we be-- not became Jews anymore. Just you and your brother, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just--

speaker: interviewer
text: You and Alex.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just me and Alex. We become instead of Alex Baum, we became Baumont. We added an O-N-T. Baumont is a French name. So we both got our identification. And the identification were made. And whoever made them, were-- they were pretty sharp people. They would always made it from towns who the German could never find any papers or anythings. Actually, ours were made, I believe, they were made out of Morocco, that we were born in Morocco. So that they couldn't-- who-- who would go to Morocco to find the-- the name. So we went-- we had our false identification. We went one morning, early, to Limoges, and took the train. And went to-- to near Lourdes-- I can't remember the town now, anymore-- to those friends. And that again, our uncle, nice uncle, gave us again 1,000 franc. And because it costed 500 franc to go-- just to pay off the-- the guard, you know, we took it. The whole-- money talks. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this guard, where was he from, this guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from that-- from that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: From that area.

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew-- he knew exactly which way to go so the German would not find us, you see. So we started out at 6 o'clock in the evening. We had a bag with some food in it. And then we had some snow shoe-- boots, you know, because we were going into deep snow. So we couldn't go walking just with shoes. That was real snow-- what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Snow boots?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Skis? Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks like a tennis racquet. You know, I don't know what you call it, anymore. But we-- we had those to put on our feet so that we wouldn't sink into the snow. Yeah. So we-- we walked and walked and walked and finally at 5 o'clock in the morning, we were in Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- who else was with you besides you and Alex and the guide?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were 10 more French young men that went with us. There was even a captain who was captured in-- from the Germans who got out of prison camp, who wanted to join again the French Army. And we were 10 of us. So we-- you know, these guys couldn't do it with two people. They-- they had to have a group together in order to do-- do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other Jews, as far as you knew on the-- with this-- you had--?

speaker: interviewee
text: How you want to ask if there's any other Jew when you're trying to deny who you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I could-- no, I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't know. Couldn't tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So we crossed into Spain. And we walked into Spain. And we came into a small town. And suddenly, we saw the police there. And we were arrested from the Spanish Police. We, just to the-- until today, we don't know why we were arrested from them. They put us in prison. And the next day, they send us back to France. We don't know why. Two uncles of mine, two weeks before, crossed the same place and nothing happens. And they went-- they went Morocco because my grandparents got a letter from them. And we didn't-- we didn't make it. That was either too many us or what it was, they send us back to-- to the borderline and as we crossed the borderline, there were six SS with four dogs to capture us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then we were arrested. So they took us to the closest village where they had a commando post. And then they started to interrogate us. My-- first came two, three guys interrogated-- were interrogated. Then came my brother was the first one to be interrogated. So when my brother was there and was asked questions, the first thing they asked him, are you a Jew? And they had him drop the pants. The first thing they looked was if he was circumcised. And so my brother said, oh, in Morocco they all get circumcised. In-- in these countries, they all-- we all get circumcised. But we didn't let them know we understood German, because they have a-- had an interpreter there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you present at that moment--

speaker: interviewer
text: It'd be a good time to stop.
